coredata.dc:title: Learning heat: High-fidelity experimental and Koo–Kleinstreuer–Li thermal conductivity predictions in nanofluids via advanced data augmentation and metaheuristic search

coredata.dc:description:
The precise forecasting of thermal conductivity in nanofluids is essential for enhancement of thermal management systems within industrial contexts. This research establishes a robust framework that combines machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), advanced augmentation, and optimization strategies to predict two types of thermal conductivity: experimental thermal conductivity (Exp-TC) and the effective thermal conductivity based on the Koo-Kleinstreuer-Li model (KKL-TC). The study curates a comprehensive dataset consisting of 278 samples, which includes a variety of nanoparticle materials (such as Cu, CuO, Al, and Al
2
O
3 ) and base fluids (including water, ethylene glycol, and transformer oil). Critical parameters, including particle size, temperature, volume fraction, thermal conductivity of particle and liquid medium, were enhanced through advanced data augmentation techniques—specifically, polynomial and fourier expansion inspired augmentation and conditional variational autoencoders to improve data diversity and the robustness of the models. The approach utilized fourteen ML and DL models in two configurations: standalone and ensemble stacking with linear regression. Optimization techniques, such as grey wolf optimization and particle swarm optimization, were employed to refine hyperparameters and enhance predictive accuracy. The results reveal that both augmentation and optimization significantly boost predictive accuracy. Specifically, CatBoost delivers the best Exp-TC (R2 = 0.99964, RMSE = 0.00464) and KKL-TC performance (R2 = 0.99782, RMSE = 0.00391). These insights establish a highly scalable and adaptable framework that extends beyond thermal system design to a broad range of engineering and industrial applications. This framework drives innovation in energy management, facilitating the development of more efficient, sustainable, and intelligent thermal systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The precise forecasting of thermal conductivity in nanofluids is essential for enhancement of thermal management systems within industrial contexts. This research establishes a robust framework that combines machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), advanced augmentation, and optimization strategies to predict two types of thermal conductivity: experimental thermal conductivity (Exp-TC) and the effective thermal conductivity based on the Koo-Kleinstreuer-Li model (KKL-TC). The study curates a comprehensive dataset consisting of 278 samples, which includes a variety of nanoparticle materials (such as Cu, CuO, Al, and Al
2
O
3 ) and base fluids (including water, ethylene glycol, and transformer oil). Critical parameters, including particle size, temperature, volume fraction, thermal conductivity of particle and liquid medium, were enhanced through advanced data augmentation techniques—specifically, polynomial and fourier expansion inspired augmentation and conditional variational autoencoders to improve data diversity and the robustness of the models. The approach utilized fourteen ML and DL models in two configurations: standalone and ensemble stacking with linear regression. Optimization techniques, such as grey wolf optimization and particle swarm optimization, were employed to refine hyperparameters and enhance predictive accuracy. The results reveal that both augmentation and optimization significantly boost predictive accuracy. Specifically, CatBoost delivers the best Exp-TC (R2 = 0.99964, RMSE = 0.00464) and KKL-TC performance (R2 = 0.99782, RMSE = 0.00391). These insights establish a highly scalable and adaptable framework that extends beyond thermal system design to a broad range of engineering and industrial applications. This framework drives innovation in energy management, facilitating the development of more efficient, sustainable, and intelligent thermal systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The dataset generated and analyzed during this study is publicly available at the GitHub repository https://github.com/AI4A-lab/Nanofluid-Thermal-Conductivity-Prediction-Dataset. Additionally, the source code used in this study is accessible on GitHub at https://github.com/AI4A-lab/Nanofluid-Thermophysical-Property-Prediction_Thermal-Conductivity_Hybrid-ML-DL.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demand for advanced thermal management solutions has intensified research into nanofluids, which combine a base fluid with two or more types of nanoparticles to enhance thermal properties. These fluids have demonstrated superior thermophysical characteristics, such as higher thermal conductivity, heat transfer efficiency, and stability, compared to conventional fluids, making them indispensable for applications in heat exchangers, cooling systems, and renewable energy systems. Accurately predicting the thermal conductivity of nanofluids is critical for optimizing their performance in industrial applications. However, the inherent complexity of the relationships between particle size, shape, concentration, temperature, and base fluid properties poses significant challenges for traditional predictive models. Machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques have emerged as transformative tools in material science and engineering due to their ability to model complex, nonlinear relationships. Huang et al. (2024) introduced interpretable deep learning models for predicting the thermal conductivity of porous materials, highlighting the importance of combining predictive accuracy with model transparency [1].

In addition to predictive modeling, optimization techniques like Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) have been widely adopted to enhance the performance of ML and DL models. This research aims to address the existing gaps by developing hybrid predictive models that leverage ML/DL techniques in combination with PSO and GWO. The proposed framework is expected to provide a systematic approach for accurately and efficiently predicting the thermal conductivity of nanofluids, thereby advancing the development of innovative thermal management technologies and contributing to the broader field of energy-efficient materials.

This research focuses on predicting the thermal conductivity of nanofluids using a dataset comprising 278 samples across twelve nanofluid combinations involving four nanoparticle materials like Copper (Cu), Copper(II) Oxide (CuO), Aluminum (Al), and Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃)—paired with three base fluids: water, ethylene glycol, and transformer oil. The key input features include particle material, base fluid, temperature (°C), particle size (nm), particle volume fraction (%), thermal conductivity of the particle (kₚ), and thermal conductivity of the liquid (kₘ). Both experimentally reported thermal conductivity values and those calculated using the Koo–Kleinstreuer–Li (KKL) model is incorporated. To address the limitation of small and imbalanced datasets—a persistent challenge in nanofluid thermal conductivity prediction—two complementary data augmentation strategies are applied. The first, Polynomial and Fourier Expansion Inspired Augmentation (PFEIA), introduces mathematically derived variations, while the second, Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAE), generates synthetic samples that preserve the statistical distribution of the original dataset. Furthermore, two nature-inspired optimization algorithms like Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) are employed to fine-tune model parameters for optimal performance. Machine learning and deep learning models are implemented in both standalone and ensemble stacking configurations, with hyperparameters optimized to maximize predictive accuracy. This research addresses the gap in integrating advanced augmentation with optimization-driven model tuning for nanofluid thermal conductivity prediction. The outcomes demonstrate that combining augmentation with metaheuristic optimization significantly improves accuracy, offering a scalable and robust predictive framework with potential applications in thermal management systems, energy-efficient cooling technologies, and advanced heat transfer equipment design.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents a comprehensive literature review, highlighting prior studies and existing approaches related to nanofluid thermal conductivity prediction. Section 3 describes the proposed methodology, including dataset characteristics, data augmentation techniques, optimization algorithms, employed machine learning and deep learning methods, and the performance metrics used for evaluation. Section 4 provides the results and discussion, comparing model performance for both experimentally reported and KKL model-based thermal conductivity values. Section 5 offers the concluding remarks, summarizing key findings and outlining potential future research directions. Finally, the list of references is provided at the end of the paper.

Recent advancements in ML and DL for thermal conductivity prediction have demonstrated significant promise across various materials and applications. Qin et al. (2023) highlighted the potential of ML techniques to predict lattice thermal conductivity by leveraging fundamental material properties, showcasing their ability to capture complex dependencies and enhance computational efficiency [2]. Huang et al. (2024) proposed an interpretable deep learning strategy for predicting the effective thermal conductivity of porous materials, emphasizing the balance between accuracy and transparency in DL models [1]. Sargam et al. (2021) similarly developed ML-based models for predicting the thermal conductivity of concrete, achieving high accuracy compared to empirical approaches, further establishing ML's versatility across domains [3]. Bang et al. (2020) demonstrated the applicability of ML techniques to compacted bentonite in nuclear energy systems, showcasing their robustness for specialized engineering applications [4]. Zhu et al. (2020) demonstrated the role of ML-driven approaches in identifying molecular structures of polymers with high thermal conductivity, highlighting ML's potential in material discovery [5]. Klochko et al. (2024) introduced transfer learning techniques for lattice thermal conductivity prediction, further expanding the scope of DL methods [6]. Zhao et al. (2022) provided a comparative analysis of multiple ML algorithms and empirical models to estimate soil thermal conductivity, demonstrating the adaptability of ML approaches across diverse applications [7]. Kanti et al. (2024) emphasized the importance of explainable ML methods for understanding the thermal conductivity and viscosity of graphene oxide-based hybrid nanofluids, highlighting interpretability as a critical factor for practical deployment [8]. Bhaumik et al. (2022) proposed a physics-aware neural network for nanofluid thermal conductivity prediction, integrating domain knowledge with data-driven approaches [9]. Amoo et al. (2024) utilized ML algorithms to analyze thermophysical properties of nanofluids, reinforcing the potential of data-driven approaches in this domain. Optimization techniques have also been instrumental in enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of ML and DL models [10]. Serrano et al. (2024) showcased advanced ML methodologies for simulating Al2O3 nanofluid heat transfer, further emphasizing the role of innovative techniques in enhancing predictive performance [11]. Zhu et al. (2020) and Zhao et al. (2022) highlighted the effectiveness of PSO and GWO in optimizing hyperparameters and feature selection, solidifying their status as essential tools in hybrid modeling frameworks [5–7]. Building on our earlier work [12], which applied machine learning and deep learning models to predict hybrid nanofluid density, this chapter shifts focus to forecasting two types of thermal conductivity. This research aims to fill the stated gaps by developing an integrated framework that leverages state-of-the-art predictive models and optimization techniques to achieve accurate, efficient, and interpretable predictions of experimental and theoretical KKL nanofluid thermal conductivity. By synthesizing insights from previous studies and employing cutting-edge methodologies, this work seeks to contribute a robust foundation for the development of next-generation thermal management solutions.

The study delineates two separate frameworks for thermal conductivity prediction: one dedicated to estimating Experimental Thermal Conductivity (Exp-TC) and the other focused on deriving effective thermal conductivity based on the Koo-Kleinstreuer-Li (KKL) model (KKL-TC). This bifurcated approach offers varied methodologies for assessing thermal conductivity, thereby enhancing its relevance across different industrial applications. The methodology encompasses multiple phases, including data collection, preprocessing, augmentation, model development, optimization, and thorough analysis of the predictive models. Fig. 1 illustrates the complete workflow of the proposed methodology, showcasing the interconnected processes involved in the framework's development.

A total of fourteen different machine learning and deep learning models are utilized in the study, with each model implemented in two configurations: (1) direct application and (2) ensemble learning through stacking with linear regression. To further enhance predictive performance, the framework incorporates two innovative data augmentation techniques—Polynomial and Fourier Expansion Inspired Augmentation (PFEIA) and Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAE). In addition, two optimization algorithms—Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)—are employed to get optimial model parameters and improve prediction accuracy.

The dataset used in this study builds upon prior work employing the Hamilton–Crosser model [13] and extends the unified dataset developed in [14], originally compiled from literature sources [15]. It comprises 278 samples across twelve nanofluid combinations involving four nanoparticle materials—Copper (Cu), Copper(II) Oxide (CuO), Aluminum (Al), and Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃)—paired with three base fluids: water, ethylene glycol, and transformer oil. Key input parameters include particle material, base fluid, temperature (°C), particle size (nm), particle volume fraction (%), thermal conductivity of particle (kₚ), and thermal conductivity of liquid (kₘ). Both experimentally reported thermal conductivity values and those calculated using the Koo–Kleinstreuer–Li (KKL) model are included.
A detailed description of the dataset construction process, sample distribution, and the KKL model formulation is provided in Appendix A.
Table 1 presents the range of values for each attribute in the dataset, ensuring comprehensive representation of the relevant parameters.

Table 2 summarizes the dataset's statistical analysis and distribution patterns. The mean experimental thermal conductivity was found to be 0.397 W/mK, which is notably lower than the mean thermal conductivity predicted by the KKL model (0.92 W/mK). Additionally, the thermal conductivity values from the KKL model exhibited low variability, with a variance of 0.008. The dataset spans a temperature range of 20 °C to 50 °C and includes particle sizes ranging from 11 nm to 150 nm.

The experimental thermal conductivity values displayed a substantial interquartile range (IQR) of 0.174 to 0.664, indicating higher variability compared to the KKL model, which showed a narrower range (0.891 to 0.982). It is important to note that the wide range of KKL-TC values (0.531 to 1.004) combined with a high mean (0.92) and low variance (0.008) is explained by the strong negative skewness (−1.847) of the distribution. This indicates that the vast majority of predicted values are tightly clustered near the maximum value, while a small number of lower-value outliers create a long tail to the left, thus extending the overall range without significantly impacting the mean or variance. Statistical metrics such as skewness and kurtosis were analyzed to evaluate the symmetry and peakedness of the data distributions. Most variables exhibited mild positive skewness, whereas the thermal conductivity values predicted by the KKL model showed significant negative skewness (−1.847), indicating a left-tailed distribution. Furthermore, the KKL model's thermal conductivity values had a kurtosis of 3.398, classifying the distribution as leptokurtic, meaning it is more peaked and has heavier tails. In contrast, other variables generally displayed platykurtic behavior, such as temperature, which had a kurtosis of −1.362, indicating flatter distributions with lighter tails.

While the KKL model's thermal conductivity values were tightly clustered, with a low variance (0.008), the experimental values were more widely dispersed, with a variance of 0.056. These discrepancies may be attributed to the theoretical nature of the KKL model, which assumes ideal conditions, whereas experimental values are influenced by additional factors such as measurement uncertainties, particle interactions, and variations in experimental conditions [16].

In the present version, the dataset has been further refined to incorporate the thermal conductivity of the particle (kp) and the thermal conductivity of the liquid (km). Additionally, a new output variable has been introduced—thermal conductivity values derived from experimental data obtained from the literature [15]. Following processing and updates, the enhanced dataset now comprises 278 data samples.

The effective thermal conductivity of nanofluids is calculated using the Koo-Kleinstreuer-Li (KKL) model, which accounts for both the static effect of particles and the dynamic effect of nanoparticle Brownian motion. The model is expressed as:

Raw datasets often contain irregularities such as noise, irrelevant entries, outliers, and missing or duplicated information, which can hinder model performance. Effective preprocessing addresses these issues by removing inconsistencies and improving data quality, thereby facilitating robust feature analysis and model development. This process typically encompasses several steps, including exploratory data analysis (EDA), data cleaning, feature engineering, and feature transformation, among others [17].

In the present study, preprocessing began with a thorough analysis of the dataset. Instances with significant missing values were removed to enhance data reliability, and outliers were identified and addressed. Duplicated entries were eliminated to prevent redundancy, ensuring a more streamlined and accurate dataset. Additionally, categorical (non-numerical) variables, such as particle material and base fluid, were transformed into numerical representations using the one-hot encoding technique [18]. One-hot encoding creates binary columns for each category within a feature, enabling the model to process categorical data effectively [19].

Following these preprocessing steps, the dataset was split into training and testing subsets. Specifically, 80 % of the data was allocated for training, providing the foundation for the model to learn and adapt to the underlying patterns within the dataset. The remaining 20 % was reserved for testing to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data. This split ensures that the model's generalizability and real-world applicability are rigorously assessed.

Machine learning algorithms typically require substantial amounts of data to achieve high levels of accuracy, resilience, and robustness in their predictions and performance [20]. Since real-world data is often noisy and exhibits significant variability, a large dataset is essential for enabling the model to learn diverse patterns and representations effectively.

To address this limitation, data augmentation has emerged as a critical preprocessing technique. Data augmentation artificially increases the size and diversity of the training dataset by applying a variety of transformations to the existing data [21]. This approach not only enhances the model's ability to generalize but also reduces the risk of overfitting by exposing the model to a wider range of scenarios.

In this study, two distinct data augmentation methods were explored to maximize the model's robustness and resilience. The first method, Polynomial and Fourier Expansion Inspired Augmentation (PFEIA), leverages mathematical transformations to introduce variations in the dataset. The second method employs Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAE), a generative modeling approach, to create synthetic data points that capture the underlying distribution of the original dataset. By applying these techniques to the numerical data in the training split, the diversity of the dataset was significantly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polynomial and fourier expansion inspired augmentation (PFEIA)
ce:section.ce:para: Polynomial and Fourier Expansion Inspired Augmentation (PFEIA) is a data augmentation technique designed to enhance numerical features within a dataset by generating new, high-order features using polynomial and Fourier series-inspired transformations. The workflow for this augmentation process is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Polynomial expansion generates higher-order terms for each numerical feature, enabling models to capture non-linear dependencies more effectively [22]. For a given feature
x i
, the polynomial expansion of degree d is expressed as follows:
(2)
x i
d
=
x i
x i 2
x i 3
…
x i d
This transformation introduces interaction terms and higher powers of the original features.

The Fourier-inspired expansion applies sine and cosine transformations to the numerical features at varying frequencies. While derived from the Fourier series, this approach is a simplified adaptation that does not strictly adhere to the formal mathematical framework of Fourier transformations [23,24]. For a numerical feature
x i
, the Fourier-inspired transformation with n terms is given by Eq. 3 as follow:
(3)
Fourier
x i
n
=
sin
n ·
x i
cos
n ·
x i
for
n = 1 , 2 , … , N
Here, N determines the number of frequency terms included in the transformation.

The choice of Polynomial and Fourier Expansion Inspired Augmentation (PFEIA) is grounded in the complex, multifactorial nature of thermal transport in nanofluids. The polynomial expansion is specifically intended to capture the significant nonlinear dependencies and interaction effects among input parameters. For instance, the influence of particle volume fraction on thermal conductivity is known to be nonlinear, often exhibiting diminishing returns at higher concentrations due to particle agglomeration. Similarly, the effect of temperature on Brownian motion and fluid viscosity is not merely linear. By generating higher-order terms (e.g., T2, ϕ) and interaction terms (e.g., T · ϕ), the polynomial expansion allows the models to learn these complex relationships directly from the data without being constrained by a predefined physical model. The Fourier-inspired expansion, on the other hand, serves as an exploratory tool to capture any potential periodic or oscillatory patterns within the feature space. While less commonly modeled, such patterns could theoretically arise from micro-convective effects or complex particle-cluster dynamics under specific conditions. PFEIA thus enriches the dataset by creating a feature space that is more representative of the underlying thermophysical phenomena.

To improve the representation learning capability of autoencoders, Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) were introduced. Unlike standard autoencoders, VAEs emphasize modeling the latent space z as a probabilistic distribution. Specifically, VAEs resample z over a defined distribution during training, as represented in Eq. 5 [26]:

Building upon VAEs, Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAEs) were developed to generate data conditioned on auxiliary information. In CVAEs, the input features x are concatenated with additional conditional variables y , such as class labels, transforming the model into a supervised learning framework [27].

The objective of training a CVAE is to maximize the evidence lower bound (ELBO), as given by Eq. 6 [28]:

This study, two nature-inspired optimization techniques were employed: Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) [30] and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) [31]. These algorithms systematically explore the parameter space to identify configurations which give optimal results. In These algorithms were applied following the augmentation of the training dataset to enhance the learning process and model accuracy.

Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) is a nature-inspired metaheuristic used for hyperparameter optimization in predictive models, imitating the hierarchical structure and hunting behavior of grey wolf packs [30]. The population is divided into four roles: alpha (α), beta (β), delta (δ), and omega (ω), reflecting varying decision-making authority. The α wolf leads the pack—not necessarily by strength but through leadership. The β wolf supports the α and communicates with lower ranks. δ wolves assist α and β in maintaining structure, while ω wolves occupy the lowest rank and follow others' decisions. This strategy balances exploration and exploitation, enabling efficient convergence to a global optimum [32,33].

The process flow of the GWO algorithm is depicted in Fig. 4 , illustrating the key phases: searching for prey, encircling the prey, attacking, and ultimately hunting the prey [30,34]. Fig. 5 demonstrates the position updating process (a) and the hierarchical structure of wolves (b). The wolves dynamically adjust their positions in response to the prey's location, represented mathematically by the prey's coordinates (x’, y’). The best agent (α), along with β and δ, has knowledge of the prey's position, which guides the position updates for all wolves.

The position of a wolf in the search space is updated as represented in Eq. 7 [34]:

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a technique originally developed for addressing nonlinear optimization problems [31,35]. It is a population-based, self-adaptive, and nature-inspired stochastic optimization algorithm. PSO has been extensively utilized to solve various optimization challenges across diverse fields [36,37].

The fundamental principle of PSO involves optimizing a problem by iteratively guiding a group of particles—representing candidate solutions—towards the most promising positions within the search space [38,39].

The Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm operates with two primary mechanisms: position update and velocity update. These processes are integral to the iterative optimization procedure. Fig. 6 provides an overview of the basic PSO algorithm. Each iteration of the PSO algorithm follows four key steps:
1.
Initialization: Particles are initialized with random positions and velocities within the defined search space.
2.
Evaluation: The objective function is evaluated at the current position of each particle. For each particle, its personal best position (P
Best ) is determined based on its performance. If the current position of a particle provides a better solution than its previous best, P
Best is updated; otherwise, the previous best position is retained.
3.
Global Best Determination: The global best position (G
Best ) is identified as the best solution achieved by any particle in the population.
4.
Velocity and Position Updates: The velocity (v) of each particle is updated based on its P
Best and G
Best , using Eq. 8 [39]:
(8)
